case_snapshot.triage_narrative: 53-year-old white male with a history of HCV/EtOH cirrhosis (on Harvoni), COPD with ongoing tobacco use, IDDM, and jugular vein thrombosis (on warfarin), presented to the ED with a chief complaint of chest pain and dyspnea. The patient was referred from a liver clinic due to a 3-day history of intermittent, productive cough with scant yellow sputum and sharp, central chest pain that occurs with coughing. The patient also reported fatigue, mild dyspnea on exertion, and wheezing. There were no symptoms of dyspnea at rest, upper respiratory symptoms, subjective fevers, chills, headache, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, dysuria, rashes, melena, hematochezia, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND), or edema. The patient walked into the ED and has allergies to codeine and penicillins. BP 119/71, P 91, RR 22, SpO2 97%, T 98.9掳 F, Pain 8.

draft_augmentation_to_review_not_treat_a.likely_working_diagnosis: Pneumonia exacerbating COPD vs. acute coronary syndrome or pulmonary embolism

ddx.diagnosis: Pneumonia exacerbating chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)
ddx.support: Intermittent productive cough with yellow sputum, wheezing, tachypnea (RR 22), and active tobacco use support a lower respiratory tract infection.
ddx.against_or_missing: No documented fever or chills.
ddx.next_discriminator: Chest X-ray, CBC, sputum culture, and bedside lung ultrasound.

ddx.diagnosis: Acute coronary syndrome (ACS) or acute cardiac ischemia
ddx.support: Sharp central chest pain and history of diabetes and cardiovascular risk factors.
ddx.against_or_missing: Chest pain is description-specific to coughing rather than exertional pressure.
ddx.next_discriminator: Electrocardiogram (ECG) and serial high-sensitivity troponin assays.

ddx.diagnosis: Pulmonary embolism (PE)
ddx.support: Active jugular vein thrombosis on warfarin represents a high hypercoagulable risk state.
ddx.against_or_missing: No acute unilateral leg swelling or pain, and oxygen saturation is normal.
ddx.next_discriminator: CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) or V/Q scan if CT is contraindicated.

teaching_points: Productive cough and chest pain in a COPD patient requires ruling out acute coronary syndrome alongside infectious exacerbation.
Vessel thrombosis history increases risk of thromboembolic events despite therapeutic anticoagulation.

case_snapshot.triage_narrative: 64-year-old white male with a history of type II diabetes mellitus, hypercholesterolemia, depression, anxiety, and chronic neck and low back pain. Recently underwent C5 corpectomy and C6-7 ACDF. He presents with difficulty swallowing, increased swelling around the surgical incision, and hoarseness. Unable to swallow pills or water, regurgitating attempts. Reports no stridor or breathing difficulties. Recently used cocaine, contributing to hypertensive urgency with BP at 180/104, headache, and chest pain. Arrived by ambulance, allergic to Tegretol.

draft_augmentation_to_review_not_treat_a.likely_working_diagnosis: Post-surgical neck hematoma or abscess concern vs. cocaine-induced hypertensive urgency

ddx.diagnosis: Post-surgical neck hematoma, edema, or abscess
ddx.support: Recent ACDF/corpectomy surgery, difficulty swallowing, surgical site swelling, and hoarseness point directly to a post-surgical compressive neck lesion.
ddx.against_or_missing: No active respiratory distress, stridor, or tracheal deviation documented.
ddx.next_discriminator: Soft tissue neck CT, direct laryngoscopy, and urgent neurosurgical consultation.

ddx.diagnosis: Cocaine-induced hypertensive urgency and chest pain
ddx.support: Recent cocaine use with BP 180/104, severe headache, and chest pain.
ddx.against_or_missing: No evidence of acute end-organ damage (e.g. pulmonary edema, active stroke findings).
ddx.next_discriminator: ECG, troponins, and observation of vitals trend.

teaching_points: Postoperative neck swelling and dysphagia after anterior cervical spine surgery is an airway emergency.
Cocaine use can mask or complicate post-surgical pain and hypertensive presentation.

draft_augmentation_to_review_not_treat_a.likely_working_diagnosis: Sepsis secondary to osteomyelitis or wet gangrene in a medically complex patient

ddx.diagnosis: Sepsis secondary to osteomyelitis or wet gangrene
ddx.support: Fever, tachycardia, hypotension, and a history of recent hospitalization for wet gangrene and osteomyelitis.
ddx.against_or_missing: No documented localized physical signs of worsening infection in the summary.
ddx.next_discriminator: Blood cultures, CBC, lactate levels, and imaging of the affected extremity.

ddx.diagnosis: Acute cardiac decompensation or uremic complications
ddx.support: End-stage renal disease, CAD, CHF, and diabetes place patient at high risk for acute fluid overload or uremia.
ddx.against_or_missing: No documented chest pain, orthopnea, or focal neurological signs.
ddx.next_discriminator: ECG, chest radiograph, electrolytes, and dialysis history verification.

teaching_points: Sepsis is a clinical diagnosis defined by infection and acute organ dysfunction; it is not a symptom.
Medically complex patients with ESRD and diabetes are highly vulnerable to rapid decompensation.

case_snapshot.triage_narrative: A 49-year-old African American male presents to the ED with a chief complaint of chest pain. He has a significant medical history, including paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (currently not on anticoagulation), cervical cord compression with associated complications, and a history of substance use (tobacco, alcohol, and IVDU on methadone). The patient describes his chest pain as sub-sternal, radiating to the left arm and jaw, with associated diaphoresis, shortness of breath, and palpitations. The pain occurs at rest, and he reports worsening orthopnea over the past two months. Vital signs are stable, and he has no known drug allergies. The patient walked into the ED and is currently not on anticoagulation despite atrial fibrillation.

draft_augmentation_to_review_not_treat_a.likely_working_diagnosis: Acute coronary syndrome vs. decompensated heart failure or endocarditis

ddx.diagnosis: Acute coronary syndrome (ACS)
ddx.support: Sub-sternal chest pain radiating to left arm/jaw, associated with diaphoresis, shortness of breath, and palpitations.
ddx.against_or_missing: Stable vital signs on arrival.
ddx.next_discriminator: ECG, high-sensitivity cardiac troponins.

ddx.diagnosis: Decompensated heart failure
ddx.support: Worsening orthopnea over two months and history of atrial fibrillation not on anticoagulation.
ddx.against_or_missing: No pedal edema or jugular venous distention documented on intake.
ddx.next_discriminator: Chest X-ray, BNP levels, and echocardiogram.

ddx.diagnosis: Infective endocarditis
ddx.support: History of intravenous drug use (IVDU) and methadone treatment presenting with chest pain/palpitations.
ddx.against_or_missing: No fever or documented heart murmur on arrival.
ddx.next_discriminator: Blood cultures, inflammatory markers, and transesophageal echocardiography.

teaching_points: IVDU history dramatically increases risk for infective endocarditis, valvular pathology, and septic emboli.
Atrial fibrillation without anticoagulation is a major stroke and embolic risk factor.

She arrives via ambulance. The patient has experienced severe hypotension and supraventricular tachycardia (SVT). She was recently intubated for airway protection and had a central line placed for vascular access. Her vital signs on arrival were BP 120/58, HR 150, RR 20, SpO2 97%, and temperature 98.8掳 F, with no reported pain. The patient has a known allergy to penicillins.

draft_augmentation_to_review_not_treat_a.likely_working_diagnosis: Septic shock secondary to severe pneumonia vs. postoperative central nervous system infection

ddx.diagnosis: Septic shock secondary to severe pneumonia
ddx.support: History of fever, presumed pneumonia, severe hypotension, and supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) requiring intubation.
ddx.against_or_missing: Blood pressure stabilized to 120/58 post-intervention.
ddx.next_discriminator: Blood and sputum cultures, lactate clearance, and chest X-ray.

ddx.diagnosis: Postoperative central nervous system infection
ddx.support: Recent metastatic cerebellar mass resection presenting with severe fever and hemodynamic instability.
ddx.against_or_missing: No neck stiffness or focal focal neurological deficits documented since intubation.
ddx.next_discriminator: Head CT followed by lumbar puncture (if safe) and CSF analysis.

teaching_points: Intubated and mechanically ventilated patients are automatically ESI 1.
Severe tachycardia (HR 150) and recent intracranial surgery require aggressive diagnostic evaluation for infection.

case_snapshot.triage_narrative: A 72-year-old female with a complex medical history including heart failure, aortic stenosis, history of strokes, COPD, and chronic urinary retention with a Foley catheter, presents to the emergency department with shortness of breath, chest pain, and bilateral pedal edema. The patient reports a one-week history of worsening shortness of breath and pedal edema, along with intermittent left-sided chest and arm pain. On the day of admission, she experienced worsening shortness of breath, requiring oxygen supplementation. The patient denies cough, fever, abdominal pain, or recent weight gain. She was transported by ambulance and has no known drug allergies.

teaching_points: Shortness of breath and pedal edema are symptoms of decompensated heart failure, which is exacerbated by valvular aortic stenosis.

The patient was transported by ambulance and has no known drug allergies. He is alert to person and place but complains of significant thirst.

ddx.diagnosis: Post-esophagectomy complication (e.g. sepsis or anastomosis leak)
ddx.support: History of esophageal cancer status post-esophagectomy, tachycardia (HR 120), and severe thirst.
ddx.against_or_missing: No fever, active chest pain, or abdominal tenderness.
ddx.next_discriminator: Complete blood count, lactate, and CT chest/abdomen with oral contrast.

teaching_points: Altered mental status is a symptom, not a diagnosis. Intoxications, trauma, and surgical complications must be differentiated.
Pinpoint pupils and slurred speech are key signs of opioid toxicity.

Her vital signs upon arrival were stable, with a heart rate of 100, respiratory rate of 16, blood pressure of 138/78, SpO2 at 98%, and temperature of 98.1掳F. The patient rated her pain at 2/10.

Open fractures require antibiotics, tetanus assessment, wound protection, neurovascular checks, and orthopedic involvement.

ddx.diagnosis: Foot sprain, contusion, or occult fracture
ddx.support: Walk-in presentation, isolated right foot pain, pain 6/10, one exam or imaging resource, and home disposition support a stable musculoskeletal complaint.
ddx.against_or_missing: No mechanism, swelling, deformity, focal tenderness, or weight-bearing status is documented.
ddx.next_discriminator: Ottawa foot criteria, focal bony tenderness, swelling, weight-bearing ability, and radiograph if indicated.

ddx.diagnosis: Early soft-tissue infection, inflammatory arthritis, or vascular/neurovascular complication requiring screen
ddx.support: Foot pain can represent non-fracture pathology, and the source lacks structured skin, joint, and distal neurovascular exam findings.
ddx.against_or_missing: No fever, open wound, erythema, swelling, neurologic deficit, vascular compromise, or inability to bear weight is documented.
ddx.next_discriminator: Focused skin and joint exam, distal pulses, sensation, capillary refill, and weight-bearing assessment.

teaching_points: A benign-appearing foot complaint still needs a focused exam before deciding it is minor.
The source supports low acuity only if the exam does not show deformity, open wound, neurovascular deficit, or inability to bear weight.

ddx.support: History of fistulizing Crohn's disease, severe anal pain (9/10), purulent/bloody discharge from abscesses, fevers, weight loss.
ddx.against_or_missing: Vitals are stable on arrival.

ddx.next_discriminator: Blood cultures, CBC, and lactate levels.

teaching_points: Anal pain in fistulizing Crohn's disease is often due to an abscess or complex fistula; it is a clinical flare, not a benign symptom.
Weight loss and episodic fevers indicate a chronic systemic inflammatory or infectious burden.
